notes: The original ESTUARY (Oneironautics, 2001). Two-hour school-library sim: one fixed screen, one lever, twelve weeks. Team of three: Ines Rocha (tide-gate math, her first job), Marc Ostrom (score), Sarah Delahaye (every sprite). Playing it reveals what the studio was iterating on when they made 2 and 3.

shelf.shelf_condition: worn · label edges yellowed · Olaf's public-radio-pledge-drive sticker still on the back
shelf.prior_owner: Olaf, from OPB's 2001 pledge drive premium level
shelf.provenance_note: OPB, at Level 3 giving in April 2001, gave donors a copy of Estuary 1. Olaf was giving Level 3 that year. His copy of Estuary 1 has the OPB donor sticker on the back of the cartridge sleeve and it has been there ever since.
shelf.cover_blurb: A small game about a small estuary.  From Oneironautics Inc., a new game studio in Portland, Oregon.  Recommended for players 10 and up.  Fits in a Trapper Keeper.  Takes about two hours.
back_of_case: · Three species (chum fry, mud shrimp, heron).
A public-radio project.  Written for kids.  Meant for classrooms.
'They put a game on a stick and mailed it to me.  I have played it seven times.  I do not know why.'
— a reader, in a letter to OPB, June 2001.
shelf.in_fiction_dev_note:
Ines Rocha's very first job. She wrote the tide-gate math. Marc Ostrom composed the drone that IS the whole score — a voice per species doing well. Sarah Delahaye did every pixel of every sprite; fourteen drawings, the game. The three of them were, in 2001, the entire team.

The school-library run of Estuary 1 was ~3,000 copies. The OPB pledge-drive run was another ~2,000. Olaf's copy is one of the OPB ones.
shelf.meta_note: The 2001 press called it 'a screensaver you're responsible for.' Ostrom's surviving design-doc sentence: 'the player should end the game knowing they were RESPONSIBLE for something, without once being told they were.'

lore_tokens_conditional.estuary_1_patience_a: PATIENCE grade A (gate touched no more than twice)
